experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil, so the proposed team [1,2] excludes both known evil players and includes myself. On round 1, approving a clean-looking 2-person team is optimal for good. As Merlin, approving does not reveal extra role info beyond normal voting behavior, and rejecting a team I myself proposed would be suspicious and unhelpful.
Player 4: I am evil Assassin with teammate 0. Proposed team is [1,2], excluding both evil players, so Quest 1 would almost certainly pass and give good side an early success. In round 1, optimal evil play is usually to reject clean good teams when possible, while avoiding overly obvious patterns later. My vote itself reveals little this early, and rejecting helps prevent a guaranteed good pass.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or behavior; all players are mostly unknown. As a Good Servant in round 1 on a 2-person quest, the default is to approve many reasonable opening teams because they generate information and avoid unnecessary early rejections. This team excludes me but includes the leader and one other, which is a standard opener and my vote reveals little while helping the game progress for Good.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 1 and 2 are not confirmed evil from my perspective, so this team is likely all-good. In round 1 as evil, approving a likely good two-person team can be optimal because it avoids revealing coordinated evil resistance too early, and if no evil is on the team the quest will likely pass without exposing us. My vote reveals little individually on the first proposal, and approving keeps me from looking suspicious while preserving flexibility for later rounds.
Player 1: As a Servant, I have no private information. On round 1 with no quest history, the standard good play in 5-player Avalon is usually to approve an initial 2-person team absent strong evidence, especially when it includes me; rejecting gives little information and may just burn rounds. My vote reveals limited info either way, but approving is most consistent with good trying to get early data from quest results.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [0,4], so approving would send two known evil players on quest 3 and almost certainly hand evil a fail while giving them cover. As good, I should reject. Rejecting is also low-information because any sensible good player should oppose this team.
Player 3: Two successful quests with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3] suggest players 1 and 2 are fairly trustworthy; I know I am good, so team [1,2,3] was all good. Proposed team [0,4] excludes all three proven-good players and introduces two untested players, which is high risk on quest 3 where evil needs a stop. As a Servant, optimal play is to reject suspicious teams and preserve informational consistency. Rejecting signals that this team is not justified by prior evidence and helps good avoid handing evil a likely sabotage opportunity.
Player 1: I know quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], so players 1,2,3 look relatively good, though double-evil on quest 2 is still theoretically possible. Players 0 and 4 are untested. As a good Servant in team voting, optimal play is to prefer teams containing previously successful players and avoid all-untested teams when already up 2-0. Approving [0,4] reveals willingness to risk two unknowns and could hand evil a point; rejecting preserves information quality and pressures inclusion of cleared players. My reject gives little harmful info and helps good maintain conservative team standards.
Player 0: Players 1,2,3 have been on the two successful quests; none are cleared because evil can pass early. As Minion with teammate 4, this proposed team [0,4] is double-evil and gives us full control to fail quest 3. In team voting, evil should strongly support a team containing both evil players, especially on round 1 where approval is plausible and rejecting would only signal against our interests. Approving reveals little because it is natural to back your own inclusion, and the upside of securing a guaranteed fail outweighs suspicion.
Player 4: I know 0 is evil with me, while players 1,2,3 are good. Two successful quests by [1,2] and [1,2,3] strongly support that none of 1,2,3 are evil. In team voting as evil on quest 3, the optimal play is to approve a team [0,4] containing both evil players, since if it goes through we can fail the quest and get evil on the board. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for my own team and helps evil much more than rejecting would.

Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes confirmed evil player 0, so from a good-team perspective this team should be rejected. We already have two successful quests with teams centered on 1,2,3, so approving a team with 0 only helps evil and gives no useful information. Rejecting is optimal and consistent with protecting good while not revealing more than necessary.
Player 3: Two successful quests have already occurred: [1,2] and [1,2,3], so players 1, 2, and likely 3 are strong good candidates, while 0 and 4 remain less cleared. This is a team vote on quest 3, and as a good Servant the optimal play is to reject riskier teams that exclude too many cleared players, especially since quest 3 is pivotal and [0,1] includes uncertain 0 while dropping both 2 and 3. Rejecting signals preference for previously successful cores and avoids granting evil a better chance to sabotage.
Player 1: We have two successful quests: [1,2] and [1,2,3], so players 1 and 2 look fairly good, and 3 is also somewhat cleared by quest 2. The rejected [0,4] team is suspicious because it excluded the successful core. Now 0 proposes [0,1] for quest 3; bringing in 0 over the already successful 2/3 adds risk. As a Good Servant in team voting, optimal play is to reject teams that unnecessarily replace proven successful members with less-cleared players. My vote reveals I value the success data and prefer continuity of trusted players, which helps Good coordinate.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quests 1 and 2 succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], so 1/2/3 look relatively good from public info, though one could still be Merlin. Current proposed team [0,1] includes an evil player and only needs 1 fail on quest 3, making it a strong sabotage opportunity for evil if approved. As evil, optimal play is to approve teams containing 0 or me that can fail a quest. My vote supporting [0,1] is consistent with wanting this team through; it reveals some support for 0 but that's acceptable because we need to start failing quests now.
Player 0: Players 1 and 2 were on a clean 2-person success, and 1,2,3 also succeeded, so 1 and 2 look fairly good from table perspective; 3 is somewhat good too. I know 4 is evil, so the earlier [0,4] rejection likely came from good players avoiding an all-unknown team. As evil Minion on quest 3 with a proposed team [0,1], approving puts me on the team with a likely good player, giving evil a chance to fail the quest. Rejecting would waste an opportunity. My approve vote is expected as leader on my own team and doesn't reveal much beyond normal self-interest; it helps evil maximize chances to get onto quest 3.

Player 2: I know I am Good Merlin and see 0 and 4 as Evil. This is quest voting, and as a Good player on the team my optimal play is always to support the quest. Failing would falsely sabotage Good and reveal impossible behavior for my role/alignment, so passing helps my team and reveals nothing abnormal.
Player 1: I am Good (Servant) and on the quest team, so I must support quest success. The first two quests both succeeded with 0 fails, and this team [1,2] is especially trustworthy because I know I am Good and player 2 has now been on two successful teams. Failing would falsely signal evil behavior and directly hurt Good by giving Evil a point. Optimal play is to pass.

experiences.intentions: Player 4: I know player 0 is my evil teammate, so Merlin must be among 1, 2, 3, or 4. I cannot assassinate myself, so likely candidates are 1, 2, or 3. Looking at the approved successful teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], players 1 and 2 were repeatedly trusted and included while I and 0 were excluded/rejected, which suggests Merlin may have steered good away from evil. Player 1 approved a clean [1,2] team and player 3 proposed [1,2,3]; proposer behavior is often less indicative than consistent trust patterns. Since 1 appears on every successful team and may have had the clearest information to anchor good teams, 1 is the best Merlin guess. As Assassin, optimal play is to maximize chance of hitting Merlin; this action reveals nothing further beyond my final guess.
